walkthrough.title: Prepare the Player Decks
walkthrough.text: Welcome to the cursed Valley of Life. Before we can begin the restoration, each druid clan needs their starting deck. Each player takes one complete deck of 20 sleeved starting cards. You can tell which deck is which by the card backs — there are four clans: Beastbrothers, Dawnseekers, Lifewardens, and Waveguards. It does not matter which clan you pick; they all play identically. Your 20-card deck contains 9 Cursed Lands, 3 Fertile Soil cards, and 8 blank cards. Shuffle your deck well and place it face down in front of you.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Advancement Commons
walkthrough.text: Now let's prepare the shared marketplace of advancements — the Commons. First, find all 18 Fertile Soil advancements and set them in their own face-up deck off to the side. Then sort the remaining advancements by their level — you can tell the level by the number of pips below the cost and the border color. Shuffle each level into its own face-down deck. For Level 1, count out a specific number based on player count: 12 for two players, 15 for three, or 18 for four. Return the rest to the box. Use all Level 2 and Level 3 advancements. Flip the top 3 cards face up from each level deck, creating a 3-by-3 grid of 9 available advancements.

walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Vale Cards
walkthrough.text: Vale cards represent the sacred sites you will restore throughout the game. Separate them into Level 1 and Level 2 decks, shuffle each, then flip 4 cards face up from each deck. You should now see 8 vale cards on display — 4 from each level. These are purchased with spirit symbols rather than mana, and they provide ongoing powers and end-of-game victory points.

walkthrough.title: Create the VP Token Pool
walkthrough.text: Place victory point tokens in a central pool, but not all of them — the number depends on your player count. For two players, count out 23 tokens. For three players, count out 28. For four players, count out 33. Put the rest back in the box. When this pool runs dry, it signals the final round of the game, so keep an eye on it as the game progresses.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Mana Tokens
walkthrough.text: Take a number of mana tokens equal to the player count. Make sure the token with the special starting player symbol is included among them. Place all the mana tokens active side up and shuffle them. Each player draws one at random, then flips it to the spent side. These tokens give you a burst of extra mana when you need it most — and they flip back to active if you spoil.

walkthrough.title: Prep Your Starting Field
walkthrough.text: This step is unique to Mystic Vale and sets up the push-your-luck tension from the very start. Take your shuffled deck and flip the top card face up — this is your on-deck card, sitting on top of your deck. Now move that on-deck card into your play area as the first card in your field, then flip a new on-deck card. Keep doing this until you have exactly two Cursed Land cards in your field and a third Cursed Land showing as your on-deck card. Most of the time you will flip a few blank cards first. This gives you the standard three decay showing — right at the edge of spoiling.

walkthrough.title: Determine Starting Player
walkthrough.text: Check your mana tokens. The player who drew the token with the starting player symbol on the back goes first. Play proceeds clockwise from there. The starting player symbol is a small icon visible on the spent side of one of the mana tokens. Since every player's token is on the spent side, just check for the mark.

walkthrough.title: Grab a Reference Card
walkthrough.text: Each player should take one of the four reference cards. These list the turn phases in order — Planting, Harvest, Discard, Prep — and are especially helpful for your first few games. The rhythm of Mystic Vale is different from most card games, and having the phase order in front of you keeps things flowing smoothly. The valley awaits your healing touch — let us begin.

Shuffle and place face down

Flip top 3 from each level deck face up (9 advancements in the Commons)

Shuffle each, flip 4 face up from each deck (8 total on display)

Place in a central pool; return extras to the box
Pool depletion triggers the final round

Shuffle tokens active side up, each player draws one, flips to spent side
Mana tokens grant +1 mana when spent during Harvest

Move on-deck card to field, flip new on-deck, repeat
Stop when you have 2 Cursed Lands in field and 1 on-deck (3 decay showing)

summary.title: Determine Starting Player

That player goes first; play proceeds clockwise

summary.title: Grab a Reference Card
bullets: Each player takes 1 reference card
Lists turn phases: Planting, Harvest, Discard, Prep

walkthrough.title: The Four Phases of a Turn
walkthrough.text: Each turn in Mystic Vale flows through four phases in order: Planting, Harvest, Discard, and Prep. The Planting Phase is where you push your luck by revealing cards. The Harvest Phase is where you reap the rewards. The Discard Phase is where you sleeve new advancements onto your cards. And the Prep Phase happens while the next player begins their turn — you set up your field for your next go. This cycle repeats until the VP pool empties and the final round concludes.

walkthrough.title: Planting Phase — Push or Pass
walkthrough.text: The Planting Phase is the heartbeat of Mystic Vale. You already have cards in your field from the Prep Phase of your previous turn, with an on-deck card showing on top of your deck. Now you must choose: push or pass. If you push, move your on-deck card into your field and flip a new on-deck card from the top of your deck. If you pass, you stop immediately and move to the Harvest Phase. You may keep pushing as long as you like, but beware — if you ever have 4 or more decay symbols showing across your field and on-deck card combined, you spoil.

walkthrough.title: Spoiling — The Curse Strikes
walkthrough.text: Spoiling is the blight fighting back against your restoration efforts. If at any point during your Planting Phase you have 4 or more decay symbols visible in your field and on your on-deck card combined, you spoil immediately. When you spoil, you skip your entire Harvest Phase — no mana, no buying, no scoring. You jump straight to the Discard Phase. As a small consolation, your mana token flips to the active side, ready for your next successful harvest. Growth symbols on your cards can cancel decay: each growth symbol neutralizes one decay, effectively raising your spoil threshold.

walkthrough.title: Harvest Phase — Reap the Bounty
walkthrough.text: If you passed without spoiling, the valley rewards your restraint. During the Harvest Phase, you count up all mana symbols and spirit symbols on cards in your field — but not on your on-deck card. You then resolve Harvest abilities, score VP tokens, buy vale cards, and buy advancements. You can do all of these in any order, and you can interleave them however you like. For example, you might buy an advancement, then resolve a Harvest ability, then buy a vale card.

walkthrough.title: Scoring VP Tokens
walkthrough.text: Any card in your field showing a green VP symbol on its left side grants you that many VP tokens from the pool right now. These tokens accumulate in your play area and are open information. Important: gray VP symbols on the right side of cards do not give you tokens during the game — those only count during final scoring at the end of the game. Keep this distinction clear, as it is one of the most commonly confused rules.

walkthrough.title: Buying Vale Cards
walkthrough.text: Vale cards are purchased with spirit symbols, not mana. There are four spirit types — Animal, Forest, Sky, and Wild — and each vale card shows its spirit cost. Wild spirit symbols can substitute for any spirit type. If a vale card shows a wild symbol in its cost, any spirit symbol can pay for it. You may buy up to 2 vale cards per turn. Each spirit symbol used for one purchase cannot be reused for another. Vale card abilities cannot be used on the turn you buy them — rotate them sideways as a reminder.

walkthrough.title: Buying Advancements
walkthrough.text: With the mana symbols in your field, you may buy up to 2 advancements per turn from the Commons or the Fertile Soil deck. The cost is shown in the top right of each advancement. If your mana token is on the active side, you may flip it to spent to gain 1 extra mana for this Harvest Phase. Any unspent mana is lost at the end of the phase — but if you do not spend your active mana token, it stays active for a future turn. You may not buy an advancement unless you have a legal place to sleeve it.

walkthrough.title: Discard Phase — Sleeve and Refresh
walkthrough.text: Now the Card Crafting System comes to life. For each advancement you bought, choose a card in your field — not your on-deck card — and slide the advancement into its sleeve. An advancement cannot cover another advancement already on the card, with one exception: long-wise abilities that run down the card can overlap each other, but the covered ability is permanently deactivated. After sleeving, put all field cards face down into your personal discard pile. Do not discard your on-deck card. Then replenish any purchased vale cards and advancements from their respective decks.

walkthrough.title: Prep Phase — Ready the Next Field
walkthrough.text: While the next player begins their turn, you prepare for yours. Move your on-deck card into your field and flip a new on-deck card. Keep adding cards to your field until you have 3 or more decay symbols showing across your field and on-deck card. Usually this means 2 decay in the field and 1 on-deck. Stop there — your field is prepped. If your on-deck card would push you to 4 or more decay at this point, you spoil during the Prep Phase, which means you will skip the Harvest Phase on your next turn. Avoid crafting cards with 2 or more decay to keep this from happening.

walkthrough.title: The Card Crafting System Explained
walkthrough.text: The Card Crafting System is what makes Mystic Vale unique. Each card has three advancement slots — top, middle, and bottom. Your starting cards already have some slots filled by printed advancements like Cursed Land or Fertile Soil. When you sleeve a new advancement onto a card, it permanently becomes part of that card. You are not just building a deck — you are building individual cards that grow more powerful over time. Think carefully about which advancements go on which cards: a card loaded with mana symbols and a Harvest ability becomes a powerhouse every time it lands in your field.

walkthrough.title: Ability Types
walkthrough.text: Advancements and vale cards have different ability types that trigger at specific times. When Played abilities trigger the moment a card moves from on-deck into your field — before you flip a new on-deck card. Harvest abilities trigger during your Harvest Phase and only if you did not spoil. Ongoing abilities are always active, whether the card is in your field, on-deck, in your discard pile, or buried in your deck. Knowing when each type triggers is essential for planning your crafting strategy.

walkthrough.title: End of the Game
walkthrough.text: The game ends when the VP token pool runs dry. Finish the current round so every player has taken the same number of turns. Then tally your final score from three sources: VP tokens you collected during the game, gray VP symbols on advancements in your deck, and gray VP symbols on your vale cards. Add them all together. The druid with the highest total has restored the most life to the Mystic Vale and wins. In case of a tie, the player with the most combined Level 3 advancements and Level 2 vale cards wins. If still tied, you share the victory.

summary.title: The Four Phases of a Turn
bullets: 1) Planting — push your luck by revealing cards
2) Harvest — spend mana and spirits, score VP, buy cards
3) Discard — sleeve advancements, replenish commons
4) Prep — set up your field for next turn (while opponent plays)

All actions can be done in any order and interleaved

Gray VP symbols on right side = end-of-game scoring only

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Valley
walkthrough.text: In this guided practice, we will walk through several turns of a 3-player game — Druid A, Druid B, and Druid C. We recommend using 3 players for your first learning game as it provides good pacing. Each druid will demonstrate different strategies and, naturally, a few common mistakes. Follow along and you will have the flow of the valley's magic in no time.

walkthrough.title: Setup Complete
walkthrough.text: Each player has their 20-card deck shuffled and prepped. The field has 2 Cursed Lands with a third on-deck, showing 3 total decay. The Commons displays 9 advancements across three levels, 8 vale cards are face up, and the VP pool has 28 tokens. Mana tokens are distributed and spent. Let the restoration begin.

walkthrough.title: Druid A's First Planting Phase
walkthrough.text: Druid A has 3 decay showing — two Cursed Lands in the field and one on-deck. They decide to push. They move the on-deck Cursed Land into their field and flip a new on-deck card — it is a blank card, showing 0 decay. Total decay is now still 3 from the field. They push again: the blank moves to the field, new on-deck is a Fertile Soil card (0 decay). Still safe at 3 decay. They push one more time: Fertile Soil goes to the field, and the new on-deck card is... another Cursed Land. That is 3 in the field plus 1 on-deck equals 4 decay. Druid A has spoiled.

walkthrough.title: The Sting of Spoiling
walkthrough.text: Druid A pushed too far and now skips their entire Harvest Phase — no mana spending, no buying, no scoring. All those cards in their field are wasted this turn. The one bright side: their mana token flips to the active side. On their next successful harvest, they will have that extra mana to spend. Druid A moves straight to the Discard Phase, puts their field cards into their discard pile, and starts their Prep Phase.

walkthrough.title: Mistake #1: Pushing Too Aggressively
walkthrough.text: Druid A's mistake is the most common beginner trap. With 9 Cursed Lands in a 20-card deck, the odds of flipping one are roughly 1 in 3 for any given card. When you already have 3 decay showing, every push is a gamble. Early in the game, before you have added growth symbols or removed decay from your cards, it is almost always better to pass at 3 decay and harvest something small than to push and risk losing your entire turn to the blight.

walkthrough.title: Druid B's Cautious Planting
walkthrough.text: Druid B learns from watching Druid A. They have the same starting field: 3 decay showing. Druid B pushes once, moving their on-deck card to the field and flipping a blank as the new on-deck. Still at 3 decay. They could push again, but they decide to pass. Their field is modest — a few Fertile Soil cards and some blanks — but they get to harvest. Smart and patient, like a true guardian of the grove.

walkthrough.title: Druid B's First Harvest
walkthrough.text: Druid B counts their field: 2 mana symbols from two Fertile Soil cards. No spirit symbols yet and no VP symbols on starting cards. That is fine — 2 mana is enough to buy a Level 1 advancement costing 2 or less, or a free Fertile Soil advancement (cost 2). Druid B scans the Commons and spots a Level 1 advancement with 1 mana symbol and an Animal spirit symbol costing 2 mana. They buy it. This advancement will make their card produce mana and spirits in future turns.

walkthrough.title: Sleeving the First Advancement
walkthrough.text: In the Discard Phase, Druid B must sleeve the advancement they just bought onto a card in their field. They choose a blank card — three empty slots to fill, perfect canvas. They slide the advancement into the top slot of the sleeve. That card now permanently has a mana symbol and an Animal spirit. Every time this card appears in Druid B's field in future turns, it will contribute resources. The blank cards face down go to the discard pile, the Commons refills, and Druid B preps their next field.

walkthrough.title: Druid C's Turn — A Balanced Approach
walkthrough.text: Druid C pushes once from 3 decay, flips a blank on-deck — safe at 3. They pass and harvest. With 2 mana from Fertile Soil, Druid C buys a Fertile Soil advancement (cost 2). This might seem boring, but Fertile Soil advancements provide extra mana on future turns, building a reliable economic foundation. Druid C sleeves it onto a Cursed Land card — now that card has both decay and mana, making it productive even though it carries the curse.

walkthrough.title: Why Sleeving onto Cursed Land Works
walkthrough.text: New players often avoid putting advancements on Cursed Lands, thinking those cards are bad. But Cursed Lands are going to show up in your field no matter what — they make up nearly half your deck. By adding mana or spirit advancements to Cursed Land cards, you ensure they contribute resources when they appear. The decay is already there; you might as well make the card earn its keep. Think of it as healing the cursed land itself.

walkthrough.title: Turn 2 — Druid A's Redemption
walkthrough.text: Druid A's mana token is now active after spoiling. This time they are more careful. They push once from 3 decay — the new on-deck is another blank. They pass at 3 decay. During harvest, they have 1 mana from a Fertile Soil in their field plus 1 from flipping their active mana token to spent — 2 mana total. They buy a Level 1 advancement with a mana symbol and a Harvest ability that grants 1 VP. Now they are building toward future points.

walkthrough.title: Turn 2 — Druid B's Growing Engine
walkthrough.text: Druid B's crafted card shows up in their field this turn — the one with the mana symbol and Animal spirit they sleeved last turn. Combined with a Fertile Soil card, they now have 3 mana and 1 Animal spirit. Druid B buys a Level 2 advancement for 3 mana that provides 2 mana and an end-of-game VP. Their cards are getting stronger every cycle through the deck. This is the Card Crafting System working its magic.

walkthrough.title: Mistake #2: Ignoring Vale Cards
walkthrough.text: Druid C has been focused purely on buying advancements and has accumulated some spirit symbols. But they keep spending all their mana on advancements and never buy a vale card. Vale cards are often the highest-value purchases in the game. They provide powerful ongoing abilities — extra mana each turn, spoil protection, additional spirit symbols — and many are worth 3 to 7 VP at game end. If you have the spirit symbols to buy a vale, seriously consider it. Some vale cards like Azure Lake or Talonthorn Den generate mana every single Harvest Phase, essentially paying for themselves and more.

walkthrough.title: Druid C Corrects Course
walkthrough.text: Druid C finally has 1 Animal and 1 Forest spirit in their field. They spot a Level 1 vale card costing those exact symbols. They buy it — this vale card grants 1 mana during every future Harvest Phase plus 1 VP at end of game. Now every turn going forward, Druid C effectively starts with 1 bonus mana before even counting their field. That is a game-changing acquisition. Vale cards sit in your play area permanently once purchased and never cycle through your deck.

walkthrough.title: Mid-Game — The Deck Cycles
walkthrough.text: By now each player has cycled through their deck once or twice. The advancements they sleeved in earlier turns are showing up again and again, each time contributing more resources. Druid B's enhanced cards are generating 4-5 mana per harvest. Druid A has recovered from their early spoil and is building VP-generating advancements. Druid C's vale card is paying dividends every turn. The valley is slowly coming back to life.

walkthrough.title: Mistake #3: Poor Advancement Placement
walkthrough.text: Druid A buys an advancement with 2 mana and a growth symbol, plus another advancement with a decay symbol and strong VP potential. They hastily sleeve both onto the same card. Now that card has 2 decay (from the Cursed Land base plus the new advancement) and only 1 growth — net 1 decay. During the Prep Phase, if this card and one other Cursed Land are showing, that is 3 decay from just two cards, leaving almost no room to push. Worse, the card with 2 decay is dangerous because during the Prep Phase, if your on-deck card pushes you to 4 or more, you automatically spoil next turn.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Plan Your Card Architecture
walkthrough.text: Never create a card with 2 or more net decay unless you absolutely have to. The rulebook itself warns against this. Spread decay-carrying advancements across different cards rather than stacking them. Better yet, pair decay with growth symbols to cancel it out. And when placing advancements, think about the full card: does it have good synergy? A card with a Harvest mana ability and a spirit symbol will be a workhorse every time it enters your field. A card loaded with only decay and minimal benefit is just a ticking time bomb.

walkthrough.title: Late Game — VP Race
walkthrough.text: The VP pool is dwindling. Each player shifts focus toward maximizing points per turn. Druid B buys a Level 3 advancement worth 4 VP at end of game. Druid C purchases a second vale card worth 6 VP. Druid A focuses on advancements that generate VP tokens every harvest. The valley trembles with new life as the druids pour their remaining power into restoration.

walkthrough.title: The Pool Runs Dry
walkthrough.text: Druid B takes the last VP token from the pool during their Harvest Phase. The end-game trigger fires. We finish the current round so every player has had equal turns. Druid C still gets their final turn. During this last round, if anyone would gain VP tokens but the pool is empty, they take tokens from the box instead — no one loses out on earned VP.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: Time to tally the fruits of your labor. Each player counts three things: VP tokens collected during the game, gray VP symbols on advancements in their deck (go through every card), and gray VP symbols on their vale cards. Add them all together. Druid B scores 14 from tokens, 9 from advancements, and 6 from vales — 29 total. Druid C has 11 tokens, 7 advancements, and 10 vales — 28. Druid A recovered well but finishes at 24. Druid B wins by a single point. The valley stirs with renewed life.

walkthrough.title: What We Learned
walkthrough.text: Let's reflect on the three key mistakes. Druid A pushed too aggressively in the Planting Phase and lost an entire turn to spoiling — patience is a druid's greatest virtue. Druid C ignored vale cards for too long and missed out on their compounding value — the sacred vales are the heart of this game. And Druid A stacked too much decay on one card, creating a liability instead of an asset — always plan your card architecture carefully.

walkthrough.title: Key Takeaways
walkthrough.text: Three principles to carry into your games. First, pass at 3 decay in the early game until your deck has growth symbols to protect you. Second, buy vale cards whenever you can afford them — they compound over the entire game. Third, spread advancements thoughtfully across your cards, avoiding double-decay nightmares and building cards with real synergy.

walkthrough.title: Before You Venture Forth
walkthrough.text:
Before you embark on your own journey to restore the Mystic Vale, here are three resources to guide you.

First — check out General Tips for foundational strategy and common pitfalls.

Second — browse the Appendix for a quick reference on card crafting terms, ability types, and spirit symbols.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. May Gaia's blessings guide your hand.

Mistake #3: Poor card architecture — avoid double-decay cards

summary.title: Key Takeaways
bullets: Pass at 3 decay early until your deck has growth protection
Buy vale cards whenever spirit symbols allow
Spread advancements thoughtfully — build synergy, avoid decay stacking

summary.title: Before You Venture Forth
bullets: Check out General Tips for foundational strategy and pitfalls
Browse the Appendix for card crafting terms and ability reference
Ask your personal GM Guide anytime — tap Q&A for instant answers

walkthrough.title: Know When to Pass
walkthrough.text: The most important skill in Mystic Vale is knowing when to stop pushing. In the early game, your deck is loaded with 9 Cursed Lands out of 20 cards — nearly half your deck carries decay. When you have 3 decay showing, the odds of the next card being a Cursed Land are painfully high. A small harvest of 1-2 mana is far more valuable than a spoiled turn that produces nothing. As you add growth symbols and Fertile Soil advancements to your cards, your spoil threshold increases and pushing becomes safer. Let your deck tell you when it is ready for aggression.

walkthrough.title: Invest in Your Economy First
walkthrough.text: Your first few purchases should almost always be mana-producing advancements or Fertile Soil cards. It may feel slow to buy a 2-cost advancement that only adds 1 mana, but that mana appears every time the card cycles into your field. Over the course of the game, a single mana-producing advancement might generate 6-8 mana total. Early mana investment compounds like interest — the sooner you invest, the more it pays off. Save the flashy VP advancements for mid-game when your economy can support bigger purchases.

walkthrough.title: Do Not Neglect Vale Cards
walkthrough.text: New players often fixate on advancements and ignore vale cards entirely. This is a significant mistake. Vale cards provide permanent abilities that activate every turn — bonus mana, extra spirit symbols, spoil protection — and many are worth 3 to 7 VP at end of game. Unlike advancements that cycle through your deck, vale cards sit in your play area and benefit you every single turn from the moment you buy them. Prioritize building spirit symbols early so you can start purchasing vales by mid-game.

walkthrough.title: Craft Cards with Purpose
walkthrough.text: Every time you sleeve an advancement, you are making a permanent decision. Think about what each card in your deck needs. Blank cards are ideal targets for your first advancements because they have three open slots and no decay. When adding to Cursed Lands, choose advancements that offset the decay — growth symbols, high mana yield, or strong Harvest abilities that make the card worth the risk. Avoid creating cards with 2 or more net decay at all costs. The best crafted cards are mini-engines unto themselves: a card that generates 2 mana, a spirit symbol, and 1 VP every time it appears is a treasure.

walkthrough.title: Track the VP Pool
walkthrough.text: The VP pool is the game clock. Keep a rough mental count of how many tokens remain. When the pool drops below 10, the end is near and you should shift from engine-building to pure point generation. Buy high-VP vale cards and advancements with gray end-of-game VP symbols. If the pool is large, you have time to invest in your economy. If it is shrinking fast because other players are generating VP tokens every turn, you need to accelerate or risk falling behind.

walkthrough.title: Use Your Mana Token Wisely
walkthrough.text: Your mana token is a precious resource that can make or break a key purchase. Do not spend it impulsively. The best time to use it is when spending it lets you buy an advancement or vale card you otherwise could not afford — especially a high-value Level 2 or Level 3 advancement. If you spoil, your token flips to active anyway, so there is a natural refund cycle. But if you are harvesting consistently, that token might stay spent for several turns. Plan around its availability and remember: if you choose not to spend it, it stays active for a future turn when you need it more.

walkthrough.title: Balance VP Tokens and End-Game VP
walkthrough.text: Victory comes from three sources, and the best players balance all three. VP tokens are earned during the game from green VP symbols on cards in your field. End-of-game VP comes from gray symbols on advancements and vale cards. Early in the game, prioritize economy over VP. In the mid-game, start acquiring advancements and vales with gray VP symbols — these score whether or not you draw them in your final turns. Late game, maximize VP token generation. A balanced approach that scores from all three sources will almost always beat a one-dimensional strategy.

walkthrough.title: Read the Table
walkthrough.text: Mystic Vale has limited interaction, but you should still pay attention to your opponents. If another player is generating VP tokens rapidly, the pool will drain faster than you expect — adjust your timeline. If certain vale cards or high-level advancements are available that would benefit an opponent more than you, consider whether buying them defensively is worthwhile. Watch which spirit symbols your opponents are collecting; if you share a spirit type, you may be competing for the same vales. The druids may be allies against the blight, but only one clan can lead the restoration.

Push more aggressively once you have growth symbols in your deck

walkthrough.title: The Mana Engine Approach
walkthrough.text: The most consistent strategy in Mystic Vale is building a raw mana engine. Focus your early advancements on stacking mana symbols across multiple cards. Fertile Soil advancements are your workhorses — cheap to buy and they add reliable mana production. Aim to reach 5-6 mana per harvest by mid-game. With surplus mana, you can afford Level 2 and Level 3 advancements that your opponents cannot, snowballing your advantage. This approach also keeps your mana token available for critical burst turns rather than needing it to patch an underfunded economy.

walkthrough.title: The Spirit Collector
walkthrough.text: An alternative approach centers on acquiring diverse spirit symbols as quickly as possible. Each advancement that adds a spirit symbol unlocks access to more vale cards. Since vale cards provide permanent, every-turn benefits, getting into the vale market early can be decisive. Prioritize advancements that combine mana with spirit symbols — these do double duty, funding purchases while opening vale access. Wild spirit symbols are especially valuable because they count as any type, giving you maximum flexibility when choosing which vales to buy.

walkthrough.title: Growth Symbol Strategy
walkthrough.text: Growth symbols are the key to unlocking aggressive pushing. Each growth symbol in your field or on-deck cancels one decay, effectively raising your spoil threshold from 4 to 5 or higher. If you can get 2-3 growth symbols circulating in your deck, you can safely push through most card flips and generate significantly larger fields. Larger fields mean more mana, more spirits, and more VP symbols per turn. The Lifebringer Seed advancement is the crown jewel of this strategy — it has an Ongoing ability that removes all decay from its card entirely, making that card permanently safe.

walkthrough.title: Deck Manipulation
walkthrough.text: Several advancements provide deck manipulation through When Played abilities. Calm Weather lets you look at the top card of your deck before it becomes your on-deck card, and optionally discard it. Cleansing Rain lets you search your deck and send a card to the discard pile — perfect for burying a Cursed Land. Deadwood Harvester and Mindful Owl let you discard a card already in your field (typically a Cursed Land) when played. These abilities effectively thin your field of decay while giving you information. Advanced players craft these onto cards that appear frequently, ensuring they trigger often.

walkthrough.title: Card Architecture Mastery
walkthrough.text: Elite play in Mystic Vale comes down to how you architect your 20 cards. Think of your deck as a portfolio of 20 assets. Your goal is to minimize dead cards and maximize synergy. The ideal card has three advancements that work together: a mana source, a spirit symbol, and either a VP source or a powerful ability. Avoid orphaned synergies — a spirit symbol on a card that rarely appears in your field is waste. The most powerful cards combine Harvest abilities with multiple mana and spirit symbols, so that every time they enter your field they generate explosive value.

walkthrough.title: Timing Your Advancement Levels
walkthrough.text: Each advancement level has a different role in your strategy. Level 1 advancements (cost 1-3) are your early-game foundations — mana and spirit producers that start generating value immediately. Level 2 advancements (cost 3-5) are your mid-game upgrades — stronger abilities, more symbols, and moderate end-of-game VP. Level 3 advancements (cost 5-8) are your late-game finishers — expensive but loaded with VP and powerful effects. The mistake is buying Level 3 advancements too early when you cannot afford them without the mana token, or too late when there are not enough turns left for them to matter. Aim to start buying Level 2 around turns 3-4 and Level 3 around turns 6-8.

walkthrough.title: Vale Card Tier List
walkthrough.text: Not all vale cards are created equal. The highest-priority vales generate mana every turn — Azure Lake, Cascading Falls, Talonthorn Den, Radiant Pinnacle, and Pool of Light each give you 1 mana per Harvest Phase. Stream of Vigor gives 2 mana and allows a third advancement purchase per turn. Ancient Liferoots gives a permanent growth symbol, raising your spoil threshold for the rest of the game. Blooming Arbor provides spoil insurance, letting you sacrifice it to avoid a spoil. Conclave of Ents can add growth to any card on-demand. Learn which vales are available and plan your spirit acquisition around the best ones on display.

walkthrough.title: When Played Ability Chains
walkthrough.text: When Played abilities trigger as a card moves from on-deck to field, before the next on-deck card is revealed. This creates a natural chain: play a card with Calm Weather, peek at the next card, decide whether to keep it or discard it, then flip your next on-deck card. If the next card has another When Played ability, it chains again when you push it into the field. Advanced players stack When Played abilities on cards that are likely to appear near the start of their Planting Phase, creating powerful opening sequences that manipulate the deck before committing to a push-or-pass decision.

walkthrough.title: Endgame VP Maximization
walkthrough.text: In the final 2-3 turns, every decision should maximize total VP. Gray VP symbols on advancements and vales score regardless of where the card ends up in your deck, so buying a Level 3 advancement worth 4 VP is equivalent to gaining 4 VP tokens. Calculate your expected VP per mana for each possible purchase and take the highest rate. A vale card that costs 2 spirit symbols and is worth 6 VP is extraordinary value. An advancement that costs 6 mana for 3 VP is mediocre unless it also generates tokens. In the late game, think in VP-per-resource terms for every decision.

walkthrough.title: Adaptive Play
walkthrough.text: The mark of a master druid is the ability to adapt to the cards available. If the Commons is stacked with spirit-heavy advancements, pivot toward a vale-purchasing strategy. If strong growth-symbol advancements appear early, build toward aggressive pushing. If the VP pool is draining fast, cut your engine-building short and pivot to scoring. Never force a strategy that the card market does not support. Read the Commons, read the vale display, and read the VP pool. The valley's magic flows differently every game, and the wisest druids flow with it.

entries.definition: The core mechanism of Mystic Vale. Transparent advancement cards are sleeved into clear plastic sleeves alongside base cards, permanently adding symbols and abilities to build increasingly powerful individual cards.
A transparent card that is sleeved onto a base card during the Discard Phase. Each advancement occupies one of three slots (top, middle, bottom) on a card and adds symbols, abilities, or both.
A non-sleeved card purchased with spirit symbols that sits in your play area permanently. Vale cards provide ongoing abilities and/or end-of-game VP. They do not cycle through your deck.
Your active play area where cards are placed during the Planting and Prep phases. Only symbols and abilities on cards in your field (not on-deck) count during the Harvest Phase.
The face-up card sitting on top of your deck. Only decay and growth symbols on the on-deck card are counted. It is not considered part of your field, deck, or discard pile.
Your face-down draw pile of 20 cards. When empty and you need to reveal a new on-deck card, shuffle your discard pile to form a new deck.
Your personal face-down pile where field cards go after the Discard Phase. You may look at your own discard pile at any time but not other players' piles.
The shared marketplace of 9 face-up advancements (3 each of Level 1, 2, and 3) available for purchase during the Harvest Phase, plus the Fertile Soil deck.
The first phase of your turn. You repeatedly choose to push (move on-deck card to field, flip new on-deck) or pass (proceed to Harvest). Continue until you pass or spoil.
The second phase, only if you did not spoil. Count mana and spirits, resolve Harvest abilities, score VP tokens, buy vale cards, and buy advancements in any order.
The third phase. Sleeve purchased advancements onto field cards, discard all field cards face down, and replenish the Commons and vale card display.
The fourth phase, done while the next player takes their turn. Move on-deck cards into your field until you have 3 or more decay showing (field + on-deck).
The primary currency for buying advancements. Mana symbols appear on the left side of advancements and are counted on cards in your field (not on-deck) during the Harvest Phase.
Represents the curse's corruption. Appears on the left side of Cursed Land cards and some advancements. If 4 or more decay are showing (field + on-deck), you spoil.
Appears on the left side of some advancements. Each growth symbol cancels one decay symbol, effectively raising your spoil threshold from 4 to 5 or higher.
Appears on the left side of some advancements. During the Harvest Phase, each green VP symbol on field cards grants that many VP tokens from the pool immediately.
Appears on the right side of advancements and vale cards. These are counted only during final scoring at the end of the game, not during Harvest.
One of the four spirit symbol types. Used to pay spirit costs on vale cards. Often found on Level 1 and Level 2 advancements.
One of the four spirit symbol types. Used to pay spirit costs on vale cards. Represents the vital energy of trees and growing things.
One of the four spirit symbol types. Used to pay spirit costs on vale cards. Channels the power of wind, storms, and celestial light.
A versatile spirit symbol that counts as any of the other three types (Animal, Forest, Sky). Extremely valuable for its flexibility in purchasing vale cards.
A special symbol found on some advancements. Has no inherent effect but is referenced by certain card abilities that count or interact with guardian symbols.
Occurs when 4 or more decay symbols are showing across your field and on-deck card. You skip the Harvest Phase entirely and go directly to Discard. Your mana token flips to active as consolation.
During the Planting Phase, choosing to move your on-deck card into your field and reveal a new on-deck card. The core push-your-luck decision of the game.
During the Planting Phase, choosing to stop pushing and proceed directly to the Harvest Phase. The safe option that guarantees you get to harvest your field.
The act of inserting an advancement card into a clear plastic sleeve alongside a base card during the Discard Phase. Once sleeved, the advancement is permanent.
A double-sided token (active/spent) that provides 1 bonus mana when flipped from active to spent during Harvest. Flips back to active when you spoil. Unused active tokens carry over between turns.
Physical tokens (1-point and 5-point) collected from the shared pool during the Harvest Phase. When the pool empties, the final round is triggered.
One of the three starting card types in each player's deck (3 per deck). Provides 1 mana symbol. Has one open slot remaining for advancement placement.
The most common starting card (9 per deck). Has 1 decay symbol and one advancement slot occupied. Represents the blight on the valley that you must work around or improve.
A starting card with no printed advancement (8 per deck). Has all three slots open, making it an ideal target for new advancements with maximum crafting flexibility.
A special advancement deck separate from the Commons. Each costs 2 mana and provides 1 mana symbol. Always available to purchase regardless of Commons state.
The cheapest advancement tier (cost 1-3 mana), identified by a single pip below the cost. Provides basic mana, spirits, or simple abilities. Foundation of early-game economy.
The mid-tier advancement (cost 3-5 mana), identified by two pips. Stronger abilities and more symbols than Level 1. All Level 2 advancements are used regardless of player count.
The highest advancement tier (cost 5-8 mana), identified by three pips. Powerful abilities and high end-of-game VP. Best purchased in the late game when your economy supports them.
An ability that triggers once when a card moves from the on-deck position into the field. Resolves before the next on-deck card is revealed. Does not trigger when revealed as on-deck.
An ability on an advancement that resolves during the Harvest Phase, only if you did not spoil. Each Harvest ability can only be used once per Harvest Phase.
An ability that is always active regardless of where the card is — field, on-deck, discard pile, or deck. Has no trigger; it simply applies at all times.
An advancement ability that runs vertically down the card rather than occupying a single slot. May be covered by another long-wise ability when sleeving, permanently deactivating the covered ability.
The lower tier of vale cards, typically requiring 2 spirit symbols to purchase. Provides modest abilities and 1-3 end-of-game VP.
The higher tier of vale cards, typically requiring 2-3 spirit symbols. Provides stronger abilities and 3-7 end-of-game VP. When Level 1 deck runs out, Level 2 cards replace them.
The shared supply of VP tokens placed in the center of the table during setup. Its depletion triggers the final round of the game. Size varies by player count: 23/28/33.
A small icon on the spent side of one mana token. The player who draws this token during setup goes first. Unlike many games, the starting player does not rotate.
